title: What is a Branch?
content: Branching means you diverge from the main line of development and continue to do work without messing with that main line. In Git, a branch is simply a lightweight, movable pointer to one of your commits. The default branch name in Git is 'master' (or increasingly 'main'). As you make commits, the branch pointer automatically moves forward to the latest commit.
insight: Branches in Git are incredibly 'cheap'—they are just a file containing the 40-character SHA-1 checksum of the commit they point to.

title: Creating a Branch
content: To create a new branch, you use the 'git branch' command followed by the name you want to give the branch. For example, 'git branch testing'. This creates a new pointer to the same commit you're currently on. It's important to note that creating a branch DOES NOT switch you to that branch; it only creates the pointer.
insight: Create a branch for every new feature or bug fix; it keeps your 'main' branch clean and stable.

title: Switching Branches: git checkout
content: To switch to an existing branch, you use the 'git checkout' command. For example, 'git checkout testing'. This moves the special 'HEAD' pointer to the 'testing' branch. When you switch branches, Git records your current state and changes the files in your working directory to match the snapshot of the branch you are switching to.
insight: Git's 'HEAD' is simply a pointer to the local branch you're currently on.

title: Shortcut for Create & Switch
content: A very common operation is to create a new branch and switch to it immediately. You can do this in one step using the 'git checkout -b <branchname>' command. In newer versions of Git, you can also use 'git switch -c <branchname>'. This is the standard way most developers start working on a new task.
insight: The '-b' stands for 'branch'—it's like saying 'create this branch and then checkout to it'.

title: Basic Merging: git merge
content: Once you've finished work on a branch and want to bring those changes back into your main branch, you use 'git merge'. First, you must checkout to the branch you want to merge *into* (usually 'main'), then run 'git merge <branchname>'. If the two branches haven't diverged, Git will perform a 'fast-forward' merge, simply moving the pointer forward.
insight: Merging is how you integrate separate lines of history back into a single path.

lab.command: git checkout main && git merge feature-alpha
lab.output:
Updating 1a2b3c4..2b3c4d5
Fast-forward
 README.md | 1 +
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

title: Three-Way Merges
content: If you've made commits on 'main' since you branched off, a fast-forward merge isn't possible. In this case, Git does a 'three-way merge' using the two branch snapshots and their common ancestor. Instead of just moving the pointer, Git creates a new 'merge commit' that has two parents, representing the combined history of both branches.
insight: A merge commit is unique because it has more than one parent commit.

title: Deleting a Branch
content: Once a branch has been merged and you no longer need the pointer, you should delete it to keep your repository organized. You can do this with 'git branch -d <branchname>'. Git is smart: if the branch hasn't been merged yet, it will warn you and refuse to delete it unless you use the capital '-D' flag to force the deletion.
insight: Deleting a branch only removes the pointer; the commits themselves stay in the database until they are eventually garbage collected.

title: Listing Branches
content: To see a list of all your local branches, run 'git branch' without any arguments. The branch you are currently on will be highlighted and marked with an asterisk (*). You can also use 'git branch -v' to see the last commit on each branch, or 'git branch --merged' to see which branches have already been integrated into your current one.
insight: Keeping track of your branches is essential when working on multiple features simultaneously.

title: Merge Conflicts: What They Are
content: A merge conflict happens when Git can't automatically decide which changes to keep. This usually occurs when the same line of the same file was changed differently in the two branches being merged. When this happens, Git stops the merge process and asks you to resolve the conflict manually before it can create the merge commit.
insight: Conflicts aren't errors; they are just Git's way of saying 'I don't want to guess and potentially break your code.'

title: Resolving Conflicts
content: When a conflict occurs, Git marks the troubled area in the file with special markers: '<<<<<<<', '=======', and '>>>>>>>'. To resolve it, you edit the file to remove these markers and choose the version of the code you want (or write a new version). Once you've resolved the conflicts in all files, you 'git add' the files to mark them as resolved and then run 'git commit' to finish the merge.
insight: Conflict resolution is a normal part of collaboration; don't be intimidated by it!
